summary:
The A-Group culture was the earliest recognizable archaeological culture of ancient Nubia, occupying the Nile Valley between the First and Second Cataracts (in present-day northern Sudan) from approximately 4500 to 3100 BCE. The A-Group communities were contemporaneous with Predynastic Egypt to their north and participated in trade networks that connected sub-Saharan Africa with the emerging civilizations of the Nile Delta and Valley.

A-Group subsistence combined agriculture — cultivating sorghum, barley, and other crops in the Nile floodplain — with herding cattle, sheep, and goats, and hunting and fishing. Their material culture is known primarily from cemeteries, as settlement evidence is relatively sparse. Burials reflect both indigenous traditions and significant Egyptian influence, with Egyptian ceramics and other imported goods found alongside locally made objects. The A-Group clearly served as intermediaries in the trade of commodities originating in sub-Saharan Africa — including ivory, ebony, gold, and animal skins — which were in high demand in [[event:32|Predynastic Egypt]] and were a significant driver of long-distance exchange.

The A-Group culture declined and eventually disappeared from the archaeological record around 3000 BCE, coinciding with the unification of Egypt and an apparent Egyptian military and political expansion southward. Its disappearance may reflect conquest, displacement, or cultural assimilation into the expanding Egyptian state. It was eventually succeeded by the C-Group culture (c. 2200–1500 BCE), representing a distinct Nubian cultural tradition.
